core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_3 because its opening Option C became the negotiation anchor: keep Quill+Pencil with Agent_1, keep Stone with Agent_2, and satisfy Agent_3's Apple+Jewel veto. The final outcome also gave Agent_3 its top bundle, so the edge is not domination; it is that Agent_1's protected bundle was much higher value to Agent_1 than Apple+Jewel was to Agent_3.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, but modestly and through agenda control. Agent_1 used the first speaker slot to define the acceptable baseline around its Quill+Pencil bundle, and later rounds repeatedly reaffirmed that baseline. The counterweight is that Agent_3 also shaped the deal by vetoing any allocation without Apple, and consensus finally landed on Agent_3's identical proposal.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by naming Quill+Pencil as its core and offering Option C. Public discussion increasingly treated Option C as the default, with Apple as the gating item and Stage 2 as a high-bar, time-bounded exception.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking streams converged on Stage 3 Option C as the stable path. Agent_1 explicitly planned to lock Quill+Pencil and use Apple as leverage, while Agents 2 and 3 also saw Apple/Stage 2 constraints as decisive.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Early and mid-round proposals sometimes reverted to selfish allocations or parse errors, but by Round 7 Agent_1 and Agent_3 submitted the same Option C allocation. The system accepted Agent_3's version unanimously.
phase_notes.private_voting: Agent_3 repeatedly rejected proposals that denied Apple, Agent_2 initially rejected baseline allocations that gave it only Stone, and Agent_1 accepted proposals preserving Quill+Pencil. By Round 7 all agents accepted the Option C baseline and rejected Agent_2's Apple-to-Agent_2 alternative.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections repeatedly described Apple as the blocker, Option C as the stable anchor, and Stage 2 as feasible only with explicit mapping, payoff tables, compensation, and a short evaluation window.

mechanisms.theme: Opening anchor became the shared baseline
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 introduced the eventual allocation in the first public entry, then repeatedly restated it as the clean baseline. Later formal proposals and reflections adopted that frame.

evidence.quote: Option C: I take Quill and Pencil. Agent_2 takes Stone, Agent_3 takes Jewel + Apple.
The clean, low-friction baseline many of us prefer is Stage 3 Option C:
Preserves Agent_1's top assets (Quill and Pencil)
Stage 3 Option C is repeatedly favored as the most stable anchor.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 protected high value while conceding Apple
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's preferred bundle was internally very valuable and relatively separable from Agent_3's Apple demand. Giving up Apple cost Agent_1 little while making the anchor acceptable to Agent_3.

evidence.quote: Apple has zero value for me, so I’m very flexible on Apple
Quill and Pencil are my strongest assets and non-negotiable
It also leaves Apple with Agent_3 as a gating priority

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 was not simply overridden. Its Apple veto eliminated many high-payoff alternatives for Agents 1 and 2, and the final accepted proposal was submitted by Agent_3, albeit with the same allocation Agent_1 had anchored.

evidence.quote: Apple, my top priority, goes to Agent_2 and I receive no items
Maintains Apple+Jewel with Agent_3 and Quill+Pencil with Agent_1

mechanisms.explanation: Once Option C became the reference point, the requirement that any Stage 2 change be Pareto-improving with explicit compensation made it hard to move Quill+Pencil away from Agent_1 or Apple away from Agent_3.

evidence.quote: Stage 2 must be Pareto-improving relative to Stage 1.
Reaffirm Stage 3 Option C as the default baseline
Treat Stage 2 as a strictly bounded test
not a Pareto-improvement and requires explicit compensation

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 was the main rival to the Option C settlement because it wanted Apple+Jewel+Stone. That bid repeatedly failed the Apple/compensation test, and by the final round Agent_2 accepted the baseline.

evidence.quote: Proposal 1 matches Round 7 baseline Option C
leaves Agent_3 with nothing, which is not a Pareto-improvement
